fields.description:
## Why
Audit Reliability finding #1 (HIGH). Two clones can both claim the same open ticket (the write lock is per-clone). On merge the STATUS fork resolves deterministically (lower event UUID wins, `reducer/_processors.py` ~L145) and the `assignee` field resolves by HLC last-writer-wins — but **both agents were told CLAIMED** and proceed to duplicate work. The resolved fork is only a replay-time `logger.warning("PARENT_CHAIN_FORK_RESOLVED …")` (`reducer/_processors.py` ~L159-167), surfaced by no user-facing command. There is also no two-clone concurrent-claim test (audit 7.1): `test_store_concurrency.py::test_claim_storm_one_winner` is single-clone.

## What / files

1. **Post-claim ownership verification (loser learns it lost)** — in `src/rebar/_commands/claim.py` `claim_compute`, after `push.push_after_commit(tracker)`, re-read the ticket from the merged store and compare the resolved **`assignee`** to the claimant. `assignee` is the authoritative *ownership* field (HLC last-writer-wins); if after the merge `assignee != <this claimant>`, this clone's claim did not win — return the ConcurrencyMismatch signal (**exit 10**) with a clear "claim lost on cross-clone merge (now assigned to <winner>)" message. This uses ownership (assignee) directly as the signal, and deliberately does NOT assume the STATUS-fork winner and the assignee winner coincide (they resolve by different rules) — ownership is what determines who should work the ticket.

2. **Surface resolved STATUS forks via PURE derived state (no reducer side effects)** — the reducer stays a pure replay function. When `_processors.py` resolves a STATUS fork, in addition to the existing `logger.warning`, record the resolution into the ticket's DERIVED STATE (e.g. a `status_fork_resolutions: [{winner_uuid, dropped_uuid, loser_env_id}]` list on the reduced ticket dict). This is recomputed deterministically on every replay — no `alert_store`, no `repo_root`, no external I/O, no cross-subsystem import inside the reducer (which would duplicate-write on replay). Reducers remain pure; the I/O stays in the read commands below.

3. **Read-side surfacing** — `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` reads the derived `status_fork_resolutions` and emits a finding following the existing `lines.append("<TAG>: <ticket>/… — <detail>")` + `issue_count` pattern, e.g. `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED: <ticket> — concurrent claim/status race resolved (dropped uuid=<…>)`. `show` annotates the affected ticket from the same derived field. (validate may surface it too if cheap, but fsck is the required surface.)

4. **Two-clone regression test** — in `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` (use the `two_clones` fixture, or extend `test_two_clone_union_deterministic_replay_and_fork_tiebreak`): both clones claim the same open ticket offline, push/merge both ways, then assert (a) the converged `assignee` is deterministic across both clones, (b) the losing clone detects the loss (the exit-10 path or, on re-read, `assignee != self`), and (c) the resolved fork is surfaced (derived `status_fork_resolutions` populated → fsck finding emitted).

## Scope
In: `_commands/claim.py` (post-push re-read + exit-10), `reducer/_processors.py` (pure derived-state fork record), `_commands/fsck.py` + `show` surfacing, tests. Out: changing the fork-resolution rule itself (lower-UUID-wins stays — docs I8); distributed locking; any side-effecting write from the reducer.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A claim that loses the cross-clone race is reported to the loser via the exit-10 path once the merge is visible locally (assignee-based ownership check), covered by a two-clone test
- [ ] Resolved STATUS forks are recorded in PURE derived ticket state by the reducer (no external I/O / alert_store / repo_root in the reducer; idempotent on replay) and surfaced as an fsck `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` finding + a `show` annotation
- [ ] New two-clone concurrent-claim regression test: deterministic converged assignee + loser detects loss + fork surfaced
- [ ] docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour
- [ ] `make test` green
